questions.question: Which statement best describes encapsulation?
options: A) Storing multiple objects in an array.
B) Hiding internal state and exposing behavior through a public interface.
C) Allowing a class to inherit from multiple superclasses.
D) Compiling code into bytecode for the JVM.
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides fields and exposes behavior via methods, protecting invariants and reducing coupling.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a class and an object?
options: A) A class runs code, while an object defines code.
B) A class is a blueprint; an object is an instance created from that blueprint.
C) A class stores data; an object stores methods.
D) There is no difference; they are synonyms.
questions.explanation: A class defines structure/behavior; an object is a concrete instance with its own state.

questions.question: Given String a = new String("hi"); String b = "hi"; which compares content correctly?
options: A) a == b
B) a.equals(b)
C) a.compareTo(b) == 1
D) a = b
questions.explanation: equals compares String content; == compares references.

questions.question: Which keyword is used in a subclass to invoke its superclass constructor?
options: A) this
B) super
C) base
D) parent
questions.explanation: super(...) must be the first statement to call a superclass constructor.

questions.question: What happens when a subclass overrides a method of its superclass?
options: A) The superclass method is permanently deleted.
B) At runtime, the method of the actual object type is called (dynamic dispatch).
C) The subclass cannot access any superclass fields.
D) The subclass must be abstract.
questions.explanation: Polymorphism ensures the most specific override is dispatched at runtime based on the object’s concrete type.

questions.question: Which is true about static members?
options: A) Static fields are copied into every object.
B) Static methods can directly access instance fields.
C) Static members belong to the class, not any instance.
D) Static members are only available in interfaces.
questions.explanation: Static members are class-level; there is a single copy per class and they are accessed via ClassName.member.

questions.question: Which declaration correctly defines an interface in Java?
options: A) abstract interface Runnable { void run() {} }
B) interface Runnable { void run(); }
C) class Runnable implements Interface { void run(); }
D) interface Runnable() { public void run(); }
questions.explanation: Interfaces declare method signatures without bodies (unless default/static).

questions.question: Consider: class A { void f() {} } class B extends A { void f() {} } A x = new B(); Which f is called by x.f()?
options: A) A.f because x is declared as A
B) B.f because the object is a B
C) Neither; this is a compile error
D) Depends on JDK version
questions.explanation: Dynamic dispatch calls the method of the object’s actual type (B), not the reference type.

questions.question: Which design choice best indicates composition over inheritance?
options: A) class Car extends Engine
B) class Engine extends Car
C) class Car { private Engine engine; }
D) interface Engine extends Car
questions.explanation: A car has-an engine, so composition (a field) is preferred over is-a (inheritance).

questions.question: Which equals implementation is most correct?
options: A) public boolean equals(Object o) { return this == o; }
B) public boolean equals(Point p) { return x == p.x && y == p.y; }
C) public boolean equals(Object o) { if (!(o instanceof Point)) return false; Point p = (Point)o; return x == p.x && y == p.y; }
D) public boolean equals() { return true; }
questions.explanation: equals must override the Object signature and handle non-Point inputs safely.
